title: Cover time statistics of one-dimensional Brownian motion under stochastic resetting
abstract: We investigate the effect of stochastic resetting on the statistics of the cover time of a one-dimensional Brownian motion with a diffusion constant $D$, confined to a finite interval of length $L$. The cover time $t_c$, defined as the minimum time required for the particle to visit every point of the interval at least once, exhibits a non-monotonic dependence on the scaled reset rate $ρ= rL^2/4D$. The scaled mean cover time $4D\langle t_c\rangle/L^2$ initially decreases with increasing $ρ$, attains a minimum at an optimal value $ρ^*$, and then increases with $ρ$, indicating an optimal resetting rate that minimizes the search duration. Furthermore, we derive an exact analytical expression for the cover time distribution, including its asymptotic limits, which agree well with numerical simulations. These results demonstrate that stochastic resetting serves as an efficient mechanism for optimizing cover-time processes in confined geometries.
summary: This work analyzes how stochastic resetting modifies the time required for a Brownian particle to explore an entire confined space (the cover time). The authors find that there is an optimal resetting rate that minimizes this search time, providing a quantitative tool for optimizing exploration processes in confined physical systems.
main_problem: The paper investigates how stochastic resetting affects the statistics of the cover time for a one-dimensional Brownian motion confined to a finite interval.
main_result: The mean cover time exhibits a non-monotonic dependence on the scaled reset rate, achieving a minimum at an optimal rate $\rho^*$, demonstrating that resetting can efficiently optimize search processes.
method: The analysis involves deriving exact analytical expressions for the cover time distribution and its moments using Laplace transforms and residue calculus.
model_or_system: The system is a single Brownian particle undergoing diffusion on the line segment $[0, L]$ with reflecting boundaries. The dynamics are modified by introducing stochastic resetting to the center of the interval at a constant rate $r$.
key_observables: Cover time ($	ext{t}_c$), mean cover time $\langle 	ext{t}_c angle$, and the probability density function (PDF) of $	ext{t}_c$.
important_parameters: Diffusion constant $D$, interval length $L$, and the scaled reset rate $\rho = rL^2/4D$.
main_assumptions: The resetting process is modeled as Poissonian, and the mathematical derivations rely heavily on Laplace transforms and residue calculus for solving the associated renewal equations.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows the scaled mean cover time decreasing, reaching a minimum at $\rho^*$, and then increasing with the scaled resetting rate $\rho$.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the non-resetting case, then introduces the stochastic resetting mechanism, and finally derives the full cover time distribution and its asymptotic limits using advanced mathematical techniques.
why_interesting: The concept of optimizing search times via external driving (resetting) is highly relevant to open quantum systems, particularly in quantum computation or quantum sensing where initialization or measurement resets are common.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Brownian motion (BM) with reflecting boundary con- dition at x = 0 and x = L, which are shown in blue. The black region indicates the space already covered by the walker up to time t, starting at x0 = L/2. The Orange region is yet to be covered. The Brownian particle is resetting at L/2 with a rate r. The cover time tc is the first time at which the entire domain becomes black.

FIG. 2. Simulation results of scaled mean cover time with scaled resetting rate ρ = rL2/4D for different values of L shown in the figure. D is kept fixed at 0.5. The solid line represents the analytical result found in Eq.(20) for different values of L. The minima is calculated at the scaled reset rate ρ∗= 1.76976, and the minimum scaled mean cover time is found as 2.30625.

FIG. 4. The PDFs of the scaled cover time for a continuous Brownian motion with RBC in log-linear scale for different values of resetting rate r at D = 0.5. The solid lines are representations of theoretical expression of the distribution function written in Eqs.(41) and (42) and closed circles are the representation of the simulation results for different values of r written in the legends for L = 51. The inset: the PDF is shown in linear scale.
FIG. 3. Plot of A1,2(ρ) and B1,2(ρ), obtained by using the expressions (35)–(36).
Fig. 4 shows an excellent agreement between the analyt- ical prediction and numerical simulations.
